title: References, identity and equality

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java reference equality tests whether references denote the same object, equals defines value equality, and equal objects must produce equal hash codes for hashed collections.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Two separately constructed Money values compare equal through equals and produce the same hash code, so a HashSet stores one logical value. Your goal is to implement and test equality and hashing for a value-like class. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Method contracts and parameters; pf data records; Constructors and invariants; String and text processing. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java reference equality tests whether references denote the same object, equals defines value equality, and equal objects must produce equal hash codes for hashed collections. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: null needs safe handling, mutable equality fields break hashed lookup, inheritance can threaten symmetry, arrays use identity equality by default, and records derive component-based behavior. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Overriding equals is enough; hashCode may remain inherited because maps compare keys with equals eventually.

includes: Distinguish object references, null, identity, value equality and hashing contracts.
Implement and test equality and hashing for a value-like class.
excludes: null needs safe handling, mutable equality fields break hashed lookup, inheritance can threaten symmetry, arrays use identity equality by default, and records derive component-based behavior.

outcomes: Implement and test equality and hashing for a value-like class.

examples.title: References, identity and equality: direct language case
examples.situation: Two separately constructed Money values compare equal through equals and produce the same hash code, so a HashSet stores one logical value.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: Two separately constructed Money values compare equal through equals and produce the same hash code, so a HashSet stores one logical value.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Implement equals and hashCode as one stable contract: equal objects must have equal hash codes, and fields used by the contract should not change while hashed.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Implement equals and hashCode as one stable contract: equal objects must have equal hash codes, and fields used by the contract should not change while hashed.

examples.title: References, identity and equality: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A key's amount is not mutated after insertion into a HashMap because changing equality/hash state can make the entry unreachable by lookup.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A key's amount is not mutated after insertion into a HashMap because changing equality/hash state can make the entry unreachable by lookup.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: null needs safe handling, mutable equality fields break hashed lookup, inheritance can threaten symmetry, arrays use identity equality by default, and records derive component-based behavior.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Overriding equals is enough; hashCode may remain inherited because maps compare keys with equals eventually.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test equality and hashing for a value-like class.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Overriding equals is enough; hashCode may remain inherited because maps compare keys with equals eventually.
misconceptions.correction: Implement equals and hashCode as one stable contract: equal objects must have equal hash codes, and fields used by the contract should not change while hashed.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Overriding equals is enough; hashCode may remain inherited because maps compare keys with equals eventually.

analogy.example: a secured service object whose constructor admits only valid state and whose public desk exposes approved behavior can introduce References, identity and equality, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: understanding encapsulation, construction, identity, equality, and records
limitations: object references, hashing, shallow immutability, and garbage collection are not physical ownership rules

Keep this boundary in view: null needs safe handling, mutable equality fields break hashed lookup, inheritance can threaten symmetry, arrays use identity equality by default, and records derive component-based behavior.
